content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

June 12, 1990

Tom Vandeveld, III
Legal Counsel
United Food and Commercial Workers
Local 135, AFL-CIO & CLC
2001 Camino del Rio South
San Diego, CA 92198

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-90-255

Dear Mr. Vandeveld:

You have requested advice on behalf of the United Food and
Commercial Workers Union Local 135 ("Local 135") and its federal
political action committee, the United Food and Commercial Workers
Union, Local 135 Active Ballot Committee, concerning the campaign
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 The facts
stated in this letter include information you provided me in
several telephone conversations prior to and since our receipt of
your letter.

QUESTIONS

1. Must the United Food and Commercial Workers Union, Local
135 Active Ballot Committee (the "Committee") file a Statement of
Organization with the California Secretary of State?

2. If so what would be the Committee's date of qualification
as a recipient committee?

3. Must the Committee file campaign statements for past
contributions to California candidates and committees?

4. Does the Committee qualify as a "broad based political
committee"? If so, when did it qualify?

5. If Local 135 decides to form a slate mailer organization,
what reporting obligations must be met?

_______________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Vandeveld, III,Tom
Page 2


     6.  If slate mailers are paid for in part by candidates,
their committees, ballot measure committees, and the slate mailer
organization, is there an allocation of monies spent between
persons or ballot measures appearing on the slate mailer?

                              CONCLUSION

     1.  The United Food and Commercial Workers Union Local 135
Active Recipient Committee must file a Recipient Committee Statement
of Organization (Form 410) with the California Secretary of State.

     2.  The Committee should show July 1, 1988, as "date
qualified as committee."

     3.  The Committee is required to file semi-annual statements
reporting its activity commencing with January 1, 1988.

     4.  The Committee will qualify as a "broad based political
committee" if it meets the criteria discussed in the following
analysis.

     5 and 6.  If Local 135 receives or is promised payments
totaling $500 in a calendar year from candidates or committees
that wish to appear on a slate mailer produced by Local 135, or
from other persons for the purpose of having specific candidates
or measures included in such a slate mailer, Local 135 will also
qualify as a slate mailer organization.

     Within 10 days of qualifying as a slate mailer organization,
Local 135 must file a Slate Mailer Organization Statement of
Organization (Form 400).  In addition, slate mailer organizations
must file semi-annual, pre-election, and late payment reports
disclosing payments received and expenditures made to produce
slate mailers.

     Under the Act, a slate mailer organization that produces and
distributes slate mailers is not considered to be making
contributions or independent expenditures to or on behalf of the
candidate or committees supported in the mailer.  Therefore,
there is no duty imposed on the slate mailer organization to
prorate payments received from the various persons or entities who
pay for the mailers.  However, an organization like Local 135
which sponsors a recipient committee and also qualifies as a slate
mailer organization must take precautions to avoid violation of
the contribution limitations.  For example, any payments made by
Local 135 at the behest of a candidate for the purpose of having the
candidate included in a slate mailer will be contributions to
the candidate.  In addition, if there is any understanding between
Local 135 or the Committee and a candidate
that other candidates or committees will be asked to participate
in the slate mailer in order to cover the costs for that
candidate, Local 135 and the Committee may be deemed to be making
contributions to the candidate.

Vandeveld, III,Tom
Page 3


FACTS


United Food and Commercial Workers Local 135 is a labor union
which was formed in 1986 as a result of a merger of United Food
and Commercial Workers Union Local 1222 and United Food and
Commercial Workers Union Local 229A.

Prior to 1984, Local 1222 formed a recipient committee to
make contributions to state and local candidates, and a Statement
of Organization (Form 410) was filed. In 1985, this committee was
terminated and a Statement of Termination (Form 415) was filed.

On June 14, 1984, the United Food and Commercial Workers
Union Local 1222 Active Ballot Committee was formed to make
contributions to federal candidates. A statement of organization
was filed with the Federal Elections Commission. Funding for the
Committee comes in the form of voluntary contributions rather than
membership dues. In addition to making independent expenditures
and contributions to or on behalf of various federal candidates
and one other federal committee, the Committee has also made
contributions to various state and local candidates. The first
contribution from the Committee to a California state or local
candidate was made on September 25, 1986. Subsequently, a
contribution of $3,000 was made on July 1, 1988, to Assemblyman
Peter Chacon. Since then, additional contributions have been made
to California candidates from Committee funds between January 1,
1989 and August 25, 1989.

In addition, between July 1, 1989 and December 31, 1989,
Local 135 made independent expenditures to support a California
city council candidate. This was from the union's general
treasury fund which receives its money from members' dues.

ANALYSIS

1 - 3. Any person or combination of persons which receives
contributions of $1,000 or more in a calendar year qualifies under
the Act as a "recipient committee." (Section 8201(a).)
Contributions are defined in the Act as payments for "political
purposes." (Section 8205.) Regulation 18215 (copy enclosed)
clarifies that a payment is made for political purposes if it is:

(1) For the purpose of influencing or attempting
to influence the action of the voters for or against the
nomination or election of a candidate or candidates, or
the qualification or passage of any measure; or

Vandeveld, III,Tom
Page 5


determine the amount of contribution. Factors relevant
to a determination that the donor has reason to know
that all or part of the payment will be used to make
expenditures and contributions include but are not
limited to the established practice of the person or
organization with respect to expenditures and
contributions and any representations made when the
payment is solicited.

The Commission has advised that a federal committee which has
not notified its contributors that it will use its funds to make
contributions or expenditures to support or oppose California
state or local candidates or committees, will qualify as a
recipient committee under Section 82013(a) during the second year
in which it engages in such activities. That is because any
contributions and expenditures made during the first calendar year
in which the committee engages in such activities establish a
"history" that puts the committee's contributors on notice that
some or all of their funds may be used for political purposes in
California. If the committee makes contributions or other
expenditures for political purposes in California totaling $1,000
or more during any subsequent calendar year, it is deemed to have
"received" contributions totaling $1,000 or more. (Enclosed is
an information sheet which describes how federal committees and
other organizations that do not receive contributions specifically
for California elections may qualify as recipient committees in
California.)

The Committee has made several contributions to California
state and local candidates and committees. The first contribution
was made on September 25, 1986. This contribution established a
history of using Committee funds for "political purposes." The
next contribution made to a California state or local candidate or
committee was for $3,000 on July 1, 1988. At this point, the
Committee is deemed under Regulation 18215(c) to have received
contributions totaling $3,000 which qualified it as a California
recipient committee pursuant to Section 82013(a). Section 8401
requires a recipient committee to file with the Secretary of State
a Statement of Organization (Form 410) within 10 days after it has
qualified as a committee. Therefore, the Committee should have
filed a Form 410 no later than July 11, 1988.

Disclosure statements must be filed to reflect all
contributions received and expenditures made once an organization
qualifies as a committee. The Committee did not qualify as a
committee in 1986. Therefore, the Committee had no filing
requirement in 1986. However, since it did qualify as a recipient
committee in 1988, in addition to the Statement of Organization
(Form 410), it must file appropriate campaign disclosure
statements (Form 420 or Form 450) covering from January 1, 1988

Vandeveld, III,Tom
Page 4


(2) Received by or made at the behest of:

(A) A candidate, unless it is clear from
surrounding circumstances that the payment was
received or made at his or her behest for personal
purposes unrelated to his or her candidacy or
status as an office holder. The term "payment"
includes the candidate's own money or property used
on behalf of his or her candidacy;

(B) A controlled committee;

(C) An official committee of a political
party, including a state central committee, county
central committee, assembly district committee or
any subcommittee of such committee; or

(D) An organization formed or existing
primarily for political purposes as defined in
subsection (a)(1), including but not limited to a
political action committee established by any
membership organization, labor union or
corporation.

(b) "Made at the behest" means a payment made
under the control or at the direction of, in
cooperation, consultation, coordination, or in concert
with, or at the request or suggestion of a candidate,
controlled committee, official committee of a political
party, or organization formed or existing primarily for
political purposes.

(c) "Contribution" includes any monetary or non-
monetary payment for which full and adequate consider-
ation is not made to the donor that is received by any
person or organization other than a candidate,
controlled committee, official committee of a political
party, or organization formed or existing primarily for
political purposes if the payment is "earmarked" for the
making of contributions or expenditures. A payment is
"earmarked" when, at the time of making the payment, the
donor knows or has reason to know that the payment or the
funds with which the payment will be commingled will be
used to make contributions, as defined in Government
Code Section 82015 and this regulation, or expenditures,
as defined in Government Code Section 82025 and 2 Cal.
Adm. Code Section 18225. If the donor knows or has
reason to know that only part of the payment will be
used to make contributions or expenditures, the payment
shall be apportioned on a reasonable basis in order to

Vandeveld, III,Tom
Page 6


through December 31, 1989.2 The Committee may file one campaign
statement for the entire year January 1, 1988 through December 31,
1988. For 1989, the Committee should file two semi-annual
statements covering January 1 - June 30, 1989, and July 1 -
December 31, 1989. Whether the Committee files Form 420 or Form
450 is determined by whether it has received contributions
totalling $100 or more during the calendar year from one
contributor. If it did receive $100 or more from one contributor,
you must file Form 420, otherwise you may use the more abbreviated
Form 450. An organization which uses only a portion of the funds
it receives for making contributions or expenditures to support or
oppose California candidates or ballot measures is required to
report as "contributions received" only that portion of its
receipts which are actually spent in connection with supporting or
opposing California state or local candidates or ballot measures.
The enclosed information sheet will assist you in determining how
to report the contributions received.

4. Regulation 18502 (copy enclosed) provides that a
committee qualifies as a "broad based political committee" when it
meets all of the following requirements: it has existed as a
California recipient committee for more than six months in the
period covered by the current fiscal year and the two previous
fiscal years it has received contributions from 100 or more
contributors in the current fiscal year and the two previous
fiscal years it has made contributions to five or more candidates.

5 and 6. An entity qualifies as a slate mailer organization
if it receives or is promised $500 or more in a calendar year for
the purpose of producing one or more slate mailers which support
or oppose four or more candidates or ballot measures. (Sections
82048.3 and 82048.4.) If the Committee decides to form a slate
mailer organization, it must file a Statement of Organization
(Form 400) within 10 days of qualifying as a slate mailer
organization. If an entity qualifies as a slate mailer
organization during the last 16 days before an election for which
it is required to file preelection statements, the organization
must file the information required to be reported in the slate
Mailer Statement of Organization within 24 hours of qualifying as
a slate mailer organization. The information must be filed by
telegram or personal delivery. In addition, the Form 400 must be
filed within 10 days of qualifying as indicated above.
(Section 84108.)


2 Section 91013 specifies that any person filing an original
statement after any deadline shall, in addition, to any other
penalties or remedies, be liable in the amount of ten dollars per
day after the deadline until the statement is filed. No liability
under this section shall exceed the cumulative amount stated in
the late statement or report, or one hundred dollars, whichever is
greater.

Vandeveld, III,Tom
Page 7


Enclosed is an information manual for slate mailer
organizations which sets out in detail the filing obligations of a
slate mailer organization.

If we can be of any further assistance please do not hesitate
to contact me at (916) 322-5662.


Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

[signature]

By: Wayne P. Imberi
Political Reform Consultant


Enclosures

embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission June 12, 1990 Tom Vandeveld, III Legal Counsel United Food and Commercial Workers Local 135, AFL-CIO & CLC 2001 Camino del Rio South San Diego, CA 92198 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-90-255 Dear Mr. Vandeveld: You have requested advice on behalf of the United Food and Commercial Workers Union Local 135 ("Local 135") and its federal political action committee, the United Food and Commercial Workers Union, Local 135 Active Ballot Committee, concerning the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 The facts stated in this letter include information you provided me in several telephone conversations prior to and since our receipt of your letter. QUESTIONS 1. Must the United Food and Commercial Workers Union, Local 135 Active Ballot Committee (the "Committee") file a Statement of Organization with the California Secretary of State? 2. If so what would be the Committee's date of qualification as a recipient committee? 3. Must the Committee file campaign statements for past contributions to California candidates and committees? 4. Does the Committee qualify as a "broad based political committee"? If so, when did it qualify? 5. If Local 135 decides to form a slate mailer organization, what reporting obligations must be met? _______________________ 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 Vandeveld, III,Tom Page 2 6. If slate mailers are paid for in part by candidates, their committees, ballot measure committees, and the slate mailer organization, is there an allocation of monies spent between persons or ballot measures appearing on the slate mailer? CONCLUSION 1. The United Food and Commercial Workers Union Local 135 Active Recipient Committee must file a Recipient Committee Statement of Organization (Form 410) with the California Secretary of State. 2. The Committee should show July 1, 1988, as "date qualified as committee." 3. The Committee is required to file semi-annual statements reporting its activity commencing with January 1, 1988. 4. The Committee will qualify as a "broad based political committee" if it meets the criteria discussed in the following analysis. 5 and 6. If Local 135 receives or is promised payments totaling $500 in a calendar year from candidates or committees that wish to appear on a slate mailer produced by Local 135, or from other persons for the purpose of having specific candidates or measures included in such a slate mailer, Local 135 will also qualify as a slate mailer organization. Within 10 days of qualifying as a slate mailer organization, Local 135 must file a Slate Mailer Organization Statement of Organization (Form 400). In addition, slate mailer organizations must file semi-annual, pre-election, and late payment reports disclosing payments received and expenditures made to produce slate mailers.
